sourceMovie.title: Trainspotting
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Trainspotting works as a drama movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The drama and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Léon: The Professional
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Léon: The Professional (1994) belongs to the same conversation as Trainspotting — both anchor their drama in crime emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Taxi Driver
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Trainspotting's drama stayed with you, Taxi Driver (1976) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The crime through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Requiem for a Dream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Trainspotting excels in drama, Requiem for a Dream (2000) doubles down on crime to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Jackie Brown
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Jackie Brown (1997) is the natural next step after Trainspotting: same drama DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and crime elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Stand by Me
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Trainspotting who respond to its drama will find Stand by Me (1986) immediately familiar. Both films share a crime sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: T2 Trainspotting
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Trainspotting fans keep landing on T2 Trainspotting (2017) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Layer Cake
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Layer Cake draws from the same drama well as Trainspotting, channeling crime in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2004 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Basketball Diaries
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Basketball Diaries (1995) captures the drama appeal of Trainspotting without simply imitating it. The crime streak running through both films is what puts The Basketball Diaries at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Filth
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of Trainspotting — that funny edge, the crime — translates cleanly into Filth (2013). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari (1920) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps Trainspotting rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Trainspotting?
faq.answer: Try Léon: The Professional, Taxi Driver, Requiem for a Dream—each shares DNA with Trainspotting while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Trainspotting?
faq.answer: Trainspotting (1996) is best described as Drama, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Trainspotting part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Trainspotting Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
